skill.name: workflow-automation
skill.description: Automate repetitive development tasks and workflows. Use when creating build scripts, automating deployments, or setting up development workflows. Handles npm scripts, Makefile, GitHub Actions workflows, and task automation.
skill.summary: Automate repetitive development tasks and workflows. Use when creating build scripts, automating dep...
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static external command findings are true positives in context because SKILL.md provides npm, Make, shell, Docker, SSH, and GitHub Actions examples. The network URL findings are documentation links, the weak cryptography hits are false positives, and no prompt injection attempt was found. The skill is publishable with a warning because generated workflow scripts can delete files, run remote commands, and touch environment files if used without review.

medium_findings.title: Workflow Examples Execute External Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill provides npm scripts, Make targets, shell scripts, Docker commands, and GitHub Actions steps. This is expected for workflow automation, but generated commands can change files, install packages, start services, and run deployments if accepted without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and semantically central to the skill. They appear legitimate, so the concern is operational risk rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Remote Deployment Commands Require Review
medium_findings.description: The deployment example runs SSH commands against staging and production hosts. This is a legitimate deployment pattern, but unsafe host names or copied commands could modify remote systems.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SSH deployment commands are directly present in the example. The risk depends on user configuration, so confidence is high for the pattern and medium for real-world harm.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Cleanup Commands Are Included
medium_findings.description: The examples include cleanup commands that remove build directories and dependencies. The skill also warns against unconfirmed destructive commands, but its examples can still delete local project files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file deletion commands and the safety warning are both visible in SKILL.md. The affected paths are project-local, so this is moderate rather than high risk.

low_findings.title: Environment File Handling Is Benign but Sensitive
low_findings.description: The setup script copies .env.example to .env only when .env is missing. No evidence found of reading, printing, or sending secrets, but environment files often contain sensitive configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly creates an environment file from an example template. The scanner's secret-access concern is partly a false positive because no credential exfiltration is present.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to public documentation for npm scripts, Make, and Husky. No evidence found that the skill sends data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are Markdown references in the References section. They are not used for network requests or data transfer.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections at the description and destructive-command warning do not correspond to cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found prose about workflow automation and destructive commands, not cryptographic operations. This strongly indicates token-based static matching.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell and CI Command Generation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs agents to produce shell scripts, npm scripts, Make targets, Docker commands, SSH deployments, and GitHub Actions workflow steps. These patterns require user review before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present across the main examples. It is appropriate for the skill purpose but still materially increases execution risk.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time repeating setup, build, test, and deployment steps by hand. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create repeatable scripts, task runners, hooks, and CI workflows.
actual_capabilities: Drafts npm scripts for build, test, lint, format, clean, Docker, and development tasks.
Creates Makefile task interfaces with help targets and local CI commands.
Outlines Husky and lint-staged hooks for pre-commit quality checks.
Builds shell script templates for setup and deployment workflows.
Produces GitHub Actions CI workflow examples for Node.js projects.
limitations: Does not execute or verify generated commands by itself.
Requires users to adapt paths, host names, service names, and package commands.
Generated cleanup and deployment commands need manual review before use.
Examples focus on Node.js, npm, Make, Docker, Husky, and GitHub Actions.

use_cases.title: Standardize Local Development
use_cases.description: Create repeatable setup, install, build, lint, and test commands for a project.

use_cases.title: Improve Pull Request Checks
use_cases.description: Add pre-commit hooks and local CI commands that catch common issues before review.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap CI Workflows
use_cases.description: Generate a starting GitHub Actions workflow for linting, type checks, tests, and coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Scripts
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small project
prompt_templates.prompt: Create npm scripts for development, build, test, lint, format, type check, and clean tasks. Explain any command assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Makefile Interface
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Makefile that wraps our npm scripts with install, dev, build, test, lint, clean, and help targets.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Quality Hooks
prompt_templates.prompt: Design Husky and lint-staged configuration for TypeScript, JSON, and Markdown files. Include safe commands and setup steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design End-to-End Automation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow across local, CI, and deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a workflow plan with npm scripts, Make targets, setup scripts, deployment checks, and a GitHub Actions CI pipeline. Flag commands that need approval before execution.

output_examples.input: A Node.js project needs repeatable build, lint, and test commands.
output_examples.output: A grouped automation plan with package scripts, a Makefile interface, and notes for adapting paths and package manager commands.

output_examples.input: A team wants pre-commit checks for TypeScript and Markdown files.
output_examples.output: A Husky and lint-staged setup plan that runs formatting, linting, type checks, and changed-file tests before commits.

output_examples.input: A project needs a first CI workflow for GitHub Actions.
output_examples.output: A CI workflow outline with checkout, Node.js setup, dependency installation, linting, type checking, tests, and coverage upload.

best_practices: Review generated shell, cleanup, Docker, and SSH commands before running them.
Keep automation idempotent so repeated runs do not damage local state.
Parameterize environments, hosts, paths, and secrets instead of hardcoding them.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated deployment commands against production without approval.
Do not include passwords, tokens, or private keys in scripts or workflow files.
Do not use broad deletion commands unless the target paths are explicit and reviewed.

faq.question: What projects does this skill fit best?
faq.answer: It fits Node.js and TypeScript projects that use npm, Make, Docker, Husky, or GitHub Actions.

faq.question: Can it run the generated automation?
faq.answer: No. It provides script and workflow guidance. Users must review and run commands in their own environment.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Can it create deployment workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It can draft deployment scripts, but production commands should require explicit review and approval.

faq.question: Does it manage secrets?
faq.answer: No. It advises against hardcoded secrets and expects environment-specific values to be configured separately.

faq.question: What should users check before adopting output?
faq.answer: Users should verify paths, package manager commands, host names, permissions, and any command that deletes files or changes servers.
